data.title: Garrett Motion: Strong Core Business Enables Pursuit Of New Opportunities
data.description:
Summary

I give a buy rating to Garrett Motion (GTX) as it has a strong core business that rides on the slower-than-expected electric vehicle transition, which creates an extended runway for traditional turbochargers. It has also shown proof that it can win contracts in new markets (electric vehicles) and could see more than expected demand in its data center business if AI adoption surges.

Company Overview

GTX sells turbochargers for gasoline and diesel vehicles. Specifically, it designs and manufactures turbochargers that force compressed air into engines to improve fuel efficiency and increase power output. GTX is also leveraging its high-speed aerodynamics expertise to penetrate the electric compressors for hybrids and hydrogen fuel cell vehicles, as well as high-voltage electric powertrain systems for zero-emission vehicles.

Slower Electric Vehicle Transition

The long-term bull case for GTX anchors on the slower transition to electric vehicles. The bearish narrative has been that the rapid increase of electric vehicle adoption would end the turbo business. But that view is apparently wrong. The shift to electric vehicles is happening much more slowly than expected. We are not transitioning directly to full electric, but hybrid first, and that means internal combustion engines [ICE], which rely heavily on turbos to meet emissions standards, are going to remain in demand for much longer than expected.

This isn't just about extending the growth runway (longer growth); it also means GTX can maintain its most profitable revenue streams for many more years. With this cash cow, it puts GTX in a much more solid position to reinvest to scale even larger, and the data suggest GTX is indeed winning. Per management, GTX is winning >50% of new business contracts (from the latest investor deck). Hence, despite the fact that the overall ICE vehicle market may be flat or slightly declining, this level of market share would mean GTX can continue to grow this segment and generate more cash.

Electric Vehicle Contract Win and a New Market in Data Centre

While the legacy core business (traditional turbo business for gasoline and diesel engines) supports earnings today, I believe the long-term earnings growth will come from other segments: electric vehicles, commercial vehicles, and data centers.

On electric vehicles, GTX is already seeing traction here. GTX has already won their first production award for a high-speed E-Powertrain with Hande, scheduled for 2027. This is not your standard motor. This is a high-speed, 3-in-1 system that is up to 40% lighter than competitor products. By leveraging GTX's core expertise in turbo, they have developed a product to spin motors faster, and they can deliver the same power in a much smaller package, which is a critical advantage for trucks, where more weight means they can carry less cargo (revenue loss).

This also tells two important things. The most important takeaways here are that (1) GTX technologies can compete head-to-head with legacy competitors and even beat them to win actual contracts, and (2) it also gives the market more confidence to start pricing in this revenue stream.

Next are data centers, which are a huge market that could really drive solid earnings growth. The recent earnings call disclosed that GTX industrial turbos are being used in backup power generators for data centers. You can think of GTX turbos as a high-pressure oxygen tank for these generators. Massive engines are naturally hard to start, but with GTX technology, air is forced into the combustion chamber to ensure they can jump from 0% to 100% power instantly. This is exceptionally useful when it comes to backup power (reliability), which is how GTX can maintain the 99.999% uptime that AI servers demand. This could be huge because of the pace of AI adoption and the sheer magnitude of how large the AI market is. The faster the adoption and the larger the market size, the more there is a need for reliable power.

This is still small for now, but if GTX can scale this up, it should help with valuation sentiment, as the market could start valuing GM as an industrial technology business rather than a traditional auto supplier, supporting a higher valuation multiple.

Valuation

Consensus estimates are not hard to beat, in my opinion. Firstly, they are expecting diesel to go into decline over the next few years, which I doubt would be the case given GTX's ability to win market share (this applies to gas as well). The commercial vehicle segment should continue to grow at mid-single digits given the stability of the end market demand (unlikely to see disruption from batteries since batteries are simply too heavy for long-haul trucking and heavy industrial use). Aftermarket should also grow low-to-mid-single-digits at the minimum given the nature of recurring demand (customers and users come back to use the same parts from GTX, and demand here grows as GTX sells more initial hardware). As for others, growth should be strong given demand potential (electric vehicles and data centers).

Suppose GTX delivers the expected adj. EPS figure by consensus ($2.30 in FY28) (which historically has been quite accurate), and multiples stay at the same level (10x forward PE); the stock is already worth $23 vs. ~$17 today. But if multiples go higher because the market sees value from the data center upside, that would make the upside more attractive.

Investment Risk

GTX faces FX headwinds. A strengthening US Dollar reduces reported revenue, while FX swings in Chinese yuan could hurt operating margins as costs go up. The main items in GTX's cost buckets, labor and energy, could pressure earnings if they see cost inflation. Lastly, any regulatory moves, such as new electric vehicle subsidies or accelerated internal combustion bans, could bring back the fear that GM is a “dying business.” This could limit the stock's valuation.

Conclusion

My view on GTX is a buy rating. GTX has a strong market position, has shown proof that they can win demand in new markets, and have a strong upside optionality from the data center exposure. Importantly, it has a strong core business that gives it cash to reinvest and compete aggressively in these new markets.

data.title: SES S.A.: Defence Demand And C-Band Optionality - This Is A Strong Buy
data.description:
This year we initiated coverage on four companies: two have delivered outstanding share-price performance (Helios Tower and Global Ship Lease), one has tracked the broader market (i.e. Campari but, on an FX-adjusted basis, has clearly outperformed), and only one is currently down (Golar LNG), though we see meaningful scope for a rebound, which is why we upgraded it to a Strong Buy Recommendation in our recent update.

Readers may also note that three of these names sit in infrastructure, a sector we continue to favour as we head into what we expect could be a more challenging equity-market backdrop in 2026. With this in mind, today we initiate coverage on SES S.A (SGBAF).

As this is an initiation of coverage, we begin with a brief overview of the company’s background and core activities. The Group is a Luxembourg-headquartered satellite operator that delivers both broadcasting and connectivity services (Fig. 1). Its infrastructure spans a multi-orbit fleet, enabling it to distribute TV and radio channels and provide managed network connectivity for enterprises (Media, Aviation, Maritime, and Fixed) and government entities (Fig. 2).

More recently, SES has materially expanded its scale by completing the acquisition of Intelsat, a transaction announced at approximately $3.1 billion (€2.8 billion) and closed on 17 July 2025.

Following the company’s Q3 2025 update, the share price sold off sharply as investors reacted negatively to the forward guidance, which, in our view, has opened a more attractive entry point from here.

Source: SES Q3 results ppt - Fig 1

Fig 2

Why are we positive?

Starting with the negative part, the company released its Q3 results in early November and announced a reset on Fiscal Year 2025 forecasts. This negatively surprised the market and hit investor confidence in revenue and EBITDA growth.

For Fiscal Year 2025, with Intelsat included, the company guides sales of €2.6–2.7 billion and adjusted EBITDA of €1.17–1.21 billion (Fig. 3). This was below the 4/5% and 10% on sales and EBITDA vs. the Wall Street consensus average. Leverage is elevated. On a conservative net-debt-to-EBITDA methodology (treating hybrids at 100%, including leases, and using an EBITDA measure that strips out non-cash items), we estimate 5.7x at end-2026 versus 3.7x on the company’s disclosure in the Q3 press release. The company reported net debt of €5.99 billion (Fig. 5); however, we add leases (€722 million), hybrids not included in the net debt calculations (€500 million), a perpetual bond (€294 million), and minority interest (approx. €70 million). Our calculation is a net debt of almost €7.5 billion. On a positive note, the reduction in CAPEX guidance is a constructive step that supports FCF.

In the Q3 results, management has also reaffirmed its target of €210 million in operating cost synergies (Fig. 4), with savings expected across several areas. The two companies have a combined contract backlog of more than 8 billion (which represents more than 2 years of annual top-line sales).

Upon closing of the transaction, SES reiterated: sales up at a low-to-mid single-digit rate, and Pro forma Adjusted EBITDA expected to grow at a mid-single-digit CAGR, including synergies. These CAGRs are estimated for 2028. So, applying a 5% EBITDA uplift to a low 2025 EBITDA guidance, we derive €1.22 billion in 2026.

Fig 3

Fig 4

Fig 5

At first glance, the analysis above may not immediately support a buy thesis; however, it is incomplete without the following additional considerations.

There is upside potential from a further monetisation of U.S. C-band spectrum. This was already seen in a precedent set during the 2020 auction. As part of the SES–Intelsat combination, legacy Intelsat shareholders retain a contingent value right entitling them to 42.5% of proceeds from the sale of up to 100 MHz of C-band spectrum in any future auction, with SES retaining the remaining 57.5%. The previous C-band generated over $81 billion in bids, of which $9.7 billion was distributed to satellite operators. At that time, SES and Intelsat captured the vast majority of these proceeds (90%). US recent news pointed to a potentially faster process this time, with a new inquiry announced earlier this year and a formal proposal expected shortly (end-2026 or early 2027). From a valuation standpoint, the 2020 auction offers a supportive reference. Indeed, assuming the same bid, total payments to SES and Intelsat will be approximately $8.8 billion. Taking 57.5% of the equity value would result in $5 billion, and applying an 18% tax rate would yield a total contribution of $4.14 billion (or €3.53 billion). Considering a market cap of €2.4 billion and net debt of €7.5 billion, this implies a 36% upside.

We believe demand from government customers will accelerate. This is related to defence and the importance of sovereign and secure connectivity. This theme has since been reinforced by public statements from several European governments, including Germany, calling for greater strategic investment in communications infrastructure. The company will be a clear beneficiary of rising defence budgets. It is key to report that the Luxembourg state is a SES main shareholder, holding a 16.67% equity stake. This will further anchor EU strategic initiatives. European satellite operators increasingly expect governments on the continent to adopt an approach similar to that of the United States: building and operating sovereign networks while simultaneously complementing them with capacity sourced from commercial operators. This view was echoed at the recent Paris Satellite Week, where industry leaders highlighted how the U.S. defence establishment is actively diversifying its communications architecture by combining in-house systems with third-party networks. This is not only a supportive opportunity in the EU but also in the US. In 2022, SES acquired Global Enterprise Solutions from Leonardo. The integration of GES with SES Government Solutions led to the creation of the SES Space & Defence unit, dedicated to delivering advanced satellite network solutions to U.S. government and Department of Defense customers. Therefore, we see upside from a business perspective.

Beyond headline outlook figures, SES has indicated that the enlarged group is targeting free cash flow of more than €1 billion by 2027/28. If delivered, this would meaningfully strengthen the capacity for higher shareholder distributions. The company has a market cap of €2.4 billion. With the FY24 results, SES proposed a final dividend of €0.25 per A-share, bringing the total Fiscal Year 2024 dividend to €0.50 per A-share (a 9.3% yield).

Valuation

At the time of writing, the company trades with an EV/EBITDA of 12.3x, which is already below the FWD sector median of 15.67x (and SES's 5-year historical average of 15.70x). However, this valuation does not include SES upside on the new auction and its FCF generation (with deleverage potential). We believe that at current levels, SES's valuation is attractive, and the market is not assuming significant near-term synergies or EBITDA growth. Currently, the company screens at a 20% FCFE yield and a 10% unlevered FCF yield. In a reverse valuation with a target 5% FCF yield, this would imply a double valuation relative to today's price. That said, applying a conservative 7.5x EV/EBITDA to our 2026 assumption (€1.22 billion) and considering a €3.53 billion upside from the 2026/2027 auction, we see an enterprise value of €12.88 billion. Adjusting for our net debt, we derive an equity value of €5.38 billion, compared to a current market cap of €2.4 billion. Our estimated share price is set at €12.1. We should also consider a 9.4% yield.

Risks

Downside risks include a lower C-band auction and any delay. Additionally, we include lower sales from Government spending and the continuing decline of Media expenditure. Following the Intelsat acquisition, SES operates with elevated leverage, which is not well reported in the company's disclosures but was taken into consideration in our analysis. Our investment case partly assumes successful operational integration and cost synergies delivered with stable customer retention. Satellite operations require significant upfront capital expenditure with long asset lifespans. Any technological changes are a key risk.

Conclusion

All in all, we believe the company is in a strong position to capitalize on higher defence expenditure from the EU as well as upside from the U.S. C-band spectrum auction. This is combined with an FCF of 10% and a well-covered dividend yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Agree Realty: A Monthly Dividend REIT Worth Accumulating
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Agree Realty Corporation (ADC) is one of the highest-quality and well-run net lease retail REITs, with properties all across the US, a diversified tenant base, and a solid track record of monthly dividend growth.

Right now, the stock still trades below its fair value despite relatively conservative estimates, with very significant investments (ramped up recently) and potential macro improvements that can support long-term growth, being an overall very solid dividend stock worth considering.

In case you don’t know them, ADC has a high-quality portfolio of net lease retail properties, with 66.7% investment-grade tenants (~15% sub-investment grade, ~18% not rated), 2603 properties, and an overall balanced exposure to companies like Walmart (WMT), Dollar General (DG), Kroger (KR), and many others.

The company has been developing rapidly despite the tough environment, recently announcing an increase in their investment guidance to $1.5 billion to $1.65 billion, which is more than 65% higher compared to 2024’s investment volumes at midpoint.

The investment-grade concentration is actually a peer-leading metric, being more than twice as good as Realty Income’s (O), for example, while the top 3 sector concentration and retail rent PSF are also among the best, which are among the reasons why this stock is generally traded at a premium. The company also maintained about 99.7% of the portfolio leased, with a weighted-average remaining lease term of 8 years.

In fact, ADC is actively using this premium, especially through their forward equity programs, which provide significant liquidity for the future while “locking in” current prices. This way, when they find the opportunity, they have the capital ready to take advantage of it without having to depend on market prices, acting as a solid hedge in case of a crash.

In Q3, ADC released yet another very solid report, with a 17.5% increase in AFFO (7.2% per share), getting to $123.1 million, backed by solid acquisitions. During the first 9 months of 2025, this AFFO grew by 13.2% (4% per share), reaching $354.8 million, although the new guidance implies solid improvements.

ADC’s AFFO guidance was improved, with a midpoint of ~$4.32 per share that would imply a ~4.4% increase YoY, which is not bad for a company of this size in this industry and environment. Based on the current number of shares outstanding, we should see about $497.23 million in AFFO (at midpoint), placing them at a P/AFFO of ~16.7.

Financially, based on ADC’s latest report, we see a very solid position for a REIT, with an overall low leverage and a very manageable amount of debt that’s well-covered by the company’s assets. The equity is far from the ~$8.3 billion market cap, but keep in mind that the assets are not only depreciated but also recorded at the price of acquisition, not reflecting their value in today’s market.

ADC also comes with virtually no debt until 2028 and comes at a ~4.07% weighted-average interest rate, which is something that I really like, as it gives them a lot of time to wait for the market to improve and refinance at better rates. This is a similar position to Getty Realty (GTY), which I own and highly recommend you check out as well if you want a ~7% yield for a different risk-reward.

Dividends

ADC currently pays a ~4.36% monthly dividend yield that grew at a ~6% CAGR from 2014 to 2024, recently increasing it by 2.3%. This costs about $343.63 million per year, for a very solid ~69.11% AFFO payout ratio.

There’s also the very interesting option of the preferred (ADC.PR.A) which currently offers a ~6.05% yield paid monthly, with a par value of $25 vs the current ~$17.57, redeemable at the company's option starting on September 17, 2026. They shouldn’t really be in a hurry to redeem it since it costs 4.25% per year, so it seems like a very solid “sleep well at night” high-yield investment to consider. And even if they redeem it in September 2026, I really wouldn’t mind a ~42% profit in less than a year plus the accumulated dividends.

As a note, this preferred equity is actually something they brag about, as it marked the lowest cost preferred equity in the net lease REIT space ever, and that was at a time when the market was still facing significant uncertainty because of the pandemic (issued in 2021).

Risks & Opportunities

Of course, interest rates will play a very important role in the company’s future and especially their potential to expand, even though they have exposure to counter-cyclical tenants, which offsets some of this risk. Further rate cuts could be great for ADC, its tenants, and especially the final consumer, potentially leading to higher discretionary budgets and lower debt costs that could incentivise more expansion in the industry.

Still, we should keep in mind that rate cuts aren’t guaranteed to take debt costs lower, as they ultimately depend on many other factors, and the current environment could even result in higher costs of debt, as the market’s opinion about the US could change from the “safe haven” it’s been for so long.

As a note, the fact that ADC trades at such a small premium to treasuries (i.e. about 20 basis points higher dividend vs 10-year notes) should also say a lot about their perceived quality in the market’s eyes since it's viewed as a relatively very low-risk asset and the market is willing to buy it for a small premium, but it can also represent a risk in case they somehow disappoint (e.g. tenant issues, bankruptcies, scandals).

Something seen in the retail industry is the significant expansion, especially from companies like Aldi (planning to become the second-largest grocer after Walmart), Publix, Kroger, Albertsons (ACI), etc. despite the weakness seen in the environment.

This comes as traditional retailers gained more and more power thanks to their private margins, which offer differentiation and allow them to negotiate much more aggressively with the food powerhouses that dominated the market for so long, limiting shelf space and potentially forcing these companies to fight for it.

What’s even more likely is the potential to see more of these companies ramp up their growth initiatives once the environment improves, which might support even better growth rates for ADC. That comes on top of an already higher investment guidance ($1.5 billion to $1.65 billion expected in 2025), potentially offering many years of growth ahead once the environment improves.

However, since the company’s tenants ultimately depend significantly on the economy, I’ll mention once again that all the tariff pressure and now uncertainty around Venezuela can affect them significantly, as they could ultimately result in higher costs for the final consumer.

Again, it’s not about who would win the military side of a conflict between the US and Venezuela, but a "war" so close to home can not only disrupt major trade routes (Venezuela or one of their allies can even cause a disaster in the Panama Canal if it wants to retaliate), but it can also affect the US’ image, potentially leading to higher debt costs as they would make the market demand higher yields to take the risk of lending to the country.

This can then translate into higher debt costs since the SOFR is based on that, meaning a worse environment for ADC and its tenants, potentially hurting the entire supply chain and pushing valuations lower (making issuing stock more expensive).

Risks Reiterated

Even though the company’s high-quality portfolio, good management, and strong balance sheet with no upcoming debt offer a solid margin of safety, the business model still relies heavily on the economy.

Especially with the uncertainty seen in the geopolitical landscape that can be marked by a potential conflict in the Caribbean and continued tariff uncertainty, the environment has room to get worse before it gets any better, potentially leading to more volatility.

Peer Comparison

Compared to peers such as Realty Income, NNN REIT (NNN), Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT), NETSTREIT (NTST), Four Corners Property Trust (FCPT), and W. P. Carey (WPC), we see the second-lowest dividend yield combined with overall high valuation metrics from every point of view.

Again, the market is pricing in the overall high quality and peer-leading margins, and the higher metrics shouldn’t be a deterrent as they can ultimately mean accretive results on a per share basis in case they want to acquire a peer using stock.

Valuation

Starting with ADC’s midpoint AFFO guidance of $497.23 million in 2025, let’s assume a 4.5% CAGR over the first 5 years (similar to the FFO estimates) thanks to their ramped-up investments and potential macro improvements, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate.

I’ll go for a WACC of 7%, well above the company's estimated 5.8% (assumptions: 6.1% cost of equity, 5.1% cost of debt), with $3.35 billion in net debt, $175.26 million in non-controlling interests and preferred equity, and 115.1 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This gets us to an enterprise value of $12.84 billion, an equity value of $9.31 billion, and an intrinsic value of $80.93 per share, which is ~12.25% above the current $72.10.

Conclusions

Overall, ADC remains a very solid company to consider, especially if you want a high-quality investment from almost every point of view, while the preferred offers a very compelling opportunity too. Again, the preferred also comes with a very attractive risk-reward, being a win-win almost no matter what they decide to do, while the common seems like a very solid option to add over the long term, especially for a pension portfolio.

data.title: Fidelity High Dividend ETF: A High-Yield Dividend Growth And Value Hedge
data.description:
FDVV is a great blend of growth and value

The debate over whether 2026 will continue to be another great year for growth or see a rotation to value is a tough outcome to fathom. On the one hand, you have the S&P 500 (SP500) trading near record forward valuations [22.5 X], but you also have near record high earnings growth for the index as well. Value ETFs remain in that 18-20X average P/E range for US equity value and dividend funds. Today I'll discuss why The Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV) might be a great hedge no matter which direction the market heads.

My current value bucket

For my personal portfolio, I like to keep my menu of options pretty clean and easy. I may modify these buckets from year to year, but this is my value bucket heading into 2026:

  • The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD)
  • FDVV
  • Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B)

While I do own other value ETFs, these are the ones that I am primarily focused on adding to in the coming year. While Berkshire Hathaway is not an index fund, I treat it as an index fund proxy without the income. It gets an investor exposure to a diversified portfolio of excellent public and private businesses within the holding structure. I'm also still buying The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF regardless of the underperformance in 2025, as rates get cut, I treat this as one of my stores of value that I rotate gains from satellite stock positions into.

The Fidelity High Dividend ETF has been yielding about 3% all year and has been one of the top performing value/dividend index funds year to date. Let's see how it stacked up against the competition for 2025:

Year to date, FDVV has been a top performer for non-active index fund ETFs with a value slant. The closest comp to FDVV, in my opinion, is The Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG), focusing on a growth and value index as well.

The index FDVV follows and the strategy

From Fidelity:

OBJECTIVE: Provide investment returns that correspond, before fees and expenses, generally to the performance of the Fidelity High Dividend Index.

STRATEGY: Normally invests at least 80% of assets in securities included in the Fidelity High Dividend Index and in depository receipts representing securities included in the index. It may lend securities to earn income for the fund.

INDEX DESCRIPTION: The Fidelity High Dividend Index is designed to reflect the performance of stocks of large- and mid-capitalization dividend-paying companies that are expected to continue to pay and grow their dividends.

The index methodology can allocate up to 10% of the index to the largest 1000 international stock universe by market cap, so it's not strictly a US based dividend and growth ETF, but somewhere around a 90/10 spread. The index also indicates that the methodology reallocates to maintain a 40% weight of the index in the highest yielding sectors. This 40% allocation helps bump the yield up to higher levels.

This index also removes any of the following:

a. Limited Partnerships b. BDCs c. ADRs d. Closed End Funds e. UITs f. Mutual Funds

FDVV's holdings

With just over 100 holdings, this is one of the more diversified value indexes. Similar to The Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF, the fund has quite a bit of tech in the top 10 with NVIDIA (NVDA) as the largest holding at 6.19%. VIG does not have NVIDIA in the top 10 but instead has Broadcom (AVGO) at the top position.

Sector weightings

Now that we know this fund does have a tech "growth" skew to it versus other value and high dividend funds, let's see how the weighting compares to the S&P 500 side by side:

FDVV sector weighting:

Now let's use The SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY) as a proxy to see where these two index fund's sector weightings diverge:

SPY sector weighting:

Sector SPY FDVV
Technology 34.75% 25.74%
Financials 13.27% 20.11%
Communication Services 10.83% 2.83%
Consumer Cyclical 10.77% 4.26%
Health Care 9.63% 3.66%
Industrials 7.49% 2.46%
Consumer Defensive 4.83% 11.76%
Energy 2.80% 8.57%
Utilities 2.28% 9.49%
Real Estate 1.83% 11.12%
Basic Materials 1.53% 0.01%
Cash & Equivalents 0.22% 0.29%
Government / Other — 0.01%

While FDVV does have a tech skew for a dividend and value fund, we can see consumer defensive, energy, utilities, and real estate have much higher weightings along with financials versus SPY. This is where the largest divergence occurs in FDVV against the broader S&P 500, and this is where the yield is juiced from.

Performance versus other dividend and value ETFs

Over longer periods of time, in this case a near 10-year backtest, VIG and FDVV have done the best of this dividend and value cohort. While all have underperformed the S&P 500 over this period, these funds have a different purpose in one's portfolio. They provide a much higher dividend yield, allowing one to use the passive income for living expenses without fretting over when to sell shares.

Over the period from January 1, 2017 to current, FDVV exhibited a lower standard deviation [13.07% v 15.17% for the S&P 500] and a much lower "worst year" including the dividend at -4.19 % versus -18.17% for SPY. Again, moving targets in retirement can be your worst enemy when implementing your safe withdrawal strategies. Dividend funds get on time or early retirees equity exposure and optionality on whether to sell shares or turn the drip off and collect the dividend. S&P 500 index funds would be a sell shares for income strategy only at this point for most portfolios, yielding a mere 1.07%.

Dividend Yield Rank ETF Dividend Yield (TTM) Expense Ratio
1 SCHD 3.82% 0.06%
2 DVY 3.63% 0.38%
3 HDV 3.23% 0.08%
4 FDVV 2.90% 0.29%
5 VYM 2.44% 0.06%
6 DGRO 2.09% 0.08%
7 VIG 1.61% 0.06%

Now when it comes to yield, FDVV sits in the middle of the pack of these dividend ETFs. At 2.9% and mostly qualified dividends, the 2.9% yield can be close to an equivalent rate of what money market funds are yielding right now on a tax equivalent basis. For example, if you are collecting this yield from a taxable brokerage and sit in the 22% tax bracket, a 2.9% dividend yield is equivalent to: 2.9%/[1-.22] = 3.72%. With money market funds [taxed at your marginal rate] and sitting at just over 4%, these types of dividend funds become more and more of a feasible option to replace interest income as rates drop. They make even more sense the higher your tax bracket versus fixed income taxed at marginal rates.

Even though I keep a few years of cash around for sequence of returns risk, I have a target goal of around 2% total yield in dividends and interest across my entire portfolio. Each time there's a rate drop on my money market funds, I find it easy to make up the difference by rotating some satellite position growth stock gains into something like SCHD or FDVV.

A summary comparison of the dividend funds in this article

If one is looking for a pure yield focus and dividend growth as a primary objective, the Dow Dividend 100, represented by SCHD, hits the top of the spectrum here with a 3.82% starting yield. It institutes a complex ranking system based on return on equity, dividend growth rates, and absolute starting yield as its primary goal.

FDVV and VIG have similar goals slightly opposed to SCHD. Both of these funds aim for dividend growth and capital appreciation growth potential, but do not focus on the high-yield aspect as much as SCHD would. VIG in particular is squarely focused on dividend growth potential rather than starting yield and ends up with the lowest yield of the group.

FDVV is a blended strategy that, while focusing on capital appreciation, also plants 40% of the capital into the highest-yielding sectors upon each rebalance. FDVV currently creates the best middle ground between lower-yielding, higher-dividend CAGR rates and an ultimate starting yield in SCHD.

Here is a comparison of the dividend growth rates across these 3 funds:

All data from Seeking Alpha

Fund Dividend Growth TTM 3 Year Dividend CAGR 5 Year Dividend CAGR
VIG 5.29% 6.18% 9.15%
FDVV 11.29% 8.44% 9.80%
SCHD 5.35% 7.06% 9.15%

As we can see right now, FDVV's strategy seems to be firing on all cylinders. The fund has the highest TTM, 3, and 5 year dividend CAGR. Even though absolute dividend growth is not the sole focus of the index it follows, the strategy is currently achieving the best dividend growth rate results and the best 5 year total return out of the 3.

The case for growth stocks in 2026

Now I'm not proposing that a value rotation is certain to occur; the allure of tech investing is still out there, and as long as earnings growth rates continue to outperform expectations for tech, cash on the sidelines will find it hard to escape FOMO-related gravity. We still have the quantum story yet to materialize. Possible margin expansion from AI chips becoming more commoditized is another story. Energy-related CAPEX being built out to match the high rates of data center CAPEX is also interesting. Debt is just starting to be issued by tech companies that remain very strong on the balance sheet. Capital for CAPEX spending is not dried up yet.

The case for a value rotation in 2026

Growth stalls out a bit and fails to consistently beat expectations. Combine this with steep rate cuts, and investors might go hunting for yield when their cash equivalent yield gets into the low 2% range or worse. With many diversified value ETFs and non-tech blue chips yielding 2-4%, investors may look to pad the portfolio income from various resources. Value stocks also tend to be debt heavy and rate cuts affect their margins more than they do tech companies.

Summary

Whether you're betting on growth or value next year, FDVV gets you exposure to both with a surprisingly high dividend yield near 3%. While VIG also has similar goals to FDVV, it also comes with a lower starting dividend at 1.6%. The higher yield gives income investors in or near retirement utilizing taxable brokerages greater optionality. I continue to buy FDVV going into 2026.

data.title: December 2025 Rebalance: WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Index
data.description:
By Matt Wagner, CFA

For many investors, the Nasdaq 100 is the default proxy for U.S. growth stocks.

Its methodology is simple: select the 100 largest non-financial companies listed on the Nasdaq exchange and weight by market cap.

But that simplicity comes with trade-offs. Because the Nasdaq 100 does not screen for fundamentals like growth or profitability, mature companies with slower growth can still make the cut, while truly high-growth firms can be excluded simply because they're not listed on Nasdaq.

A simple comparison helps illustrate this.

Company A vs. Company B

Consider two companies: "Company A" and "Company B." Both are real companies, and neither is in the Nasdaq 100 today. Their growth profiles over time look very different:

  • In the early 2000s, both looked like fast-growing businesses.
  • Over time, Company A's growth stalled and eventually turned negative.
  • Company B's growth moderated but remained steady in the mid-single digits.
  • In the past five years, Company A has enjoyed a resurgence, with sales accelerating above 20%.

All else equal, one would assume that Company A would be the clear Nasdaq 100 candidate between the two.

Rolling Five-Year Annualized Sales Growth: Company A vs. Company B

Here's the twist:

Just one of these companies appears to be knocking on the door of the Nasdaq 100, and it's Company B: Walmart.

Company A, Eli Lilly, is listed on the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) and therefore ineligible for inclusion in the Nasdaq 100.

Why Walmart May Join the Nasdaq 100

Being included in the Nasdaq 100 carries weight. A significant amount of assets track or benchmark to the index, which makes exchange selection increasingly important for large companies.

Walmart recently shifted its listing from the NYSE to Nasdaq, effective December 9. With a market cap around $800 billion, it is positioned to become a top 10 holding in the Nasdaq 100 once it qualifies.

An example like this highlights a broader point: a growth benchmark based solely on exchange listing and market cap may miss the companies that fundamentally represent "growth."

The WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Index (WTQGRW) takes a different approach, one grounded in fundamentals.

Since launching three years ago, WTQGRW has outperformed the Nasdaq 100 by over 16% cumulatively, more than 300 basis points (bps) annualized.

Cumulative Index Total Returns since 11/30/22

A More Targeted Growth Approach: The WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Index

The Index begins with the largest 500 U.S. companies (across any major U.S. exchange) and ranks them on two equally weighted pillars:

Growth Factor

  • 50%: Median analyst earnings growth forecast
  • 25%: Trailing 5-year EBITDA growth
  • 25%: Trailing 5-year sales growth

Quality Factor

  • 50%: Trailing 3-year average return on equity
  • 50%: Trailing 3-year average return on assets

The top 100 companies, those in the highest composite quintile, form the Index, which is weighted by market cap.

Investment Process

The result is a high-conviction, concentrated growth portfolio, with 62% of Index weight in its top 10 holdings.

At the latest rebalance, the Index held an approximately 3% weight in Eli Lilly, while Walmart was not included due to its lower growth and quality profile.

WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Index: Top 10 Holdings

December Semi-Annual Rebalance: What's Changing?

Like the Nasdaq 100, WTQGRW holds 100 securities. Forty names appear in both indexes, resulting in a 62% overlap score. But the growth characteristics diverge meaningfully.

When we examine forward three-year earnings growth estimates:

  • WTQGRW's median growth rate is more than 400 bps higher than the Nasdaq 100.
  • Companies included in WTQGRW but excluded from the Nasdaq 100 have a median growth rate of 18.7%.
  • Companies in the Nasdaq 100 but not in WTQGRW have a median growth rate of just 10.1%.

These meaningful differences in growth rates reinforce the impact of WisdomTree's fundamentals-driven stock selection process.

Forward Three-Year Earnings Growth

Why a Fundamentals-Based Rebalance Matters

Each June and December, the Index systematically refreshes its exposure to high-quality, high-growth companies. These fundamental updates lead to meaningful improvements relative to broad benchmarks:

  • Return on assets is over three times that of the S&P 500.
  • Trailing five-year sales growth exceeds the S&P 500 by more than 600 bps.

This disciplined process keeps the Index aligned with the companies that define quality growth, not just the largest companies in the market.

Index Characteristics

Conclusion

As the Walmart example shows, relying solely on exchange rules and market cap can create a very different picture of “growth” than what fundamentals would indicate.

The WisdomTree U.S. Quality Growth Index takes a more deliberate approach, systematically targeting companies with the strongest blend of quality and growth and refreshing that exposure every six months. The result is an Index that delivers the type of growth exposure investors expect, rather than one that simply includes the largest companies based on where they happen to trade.

Matt Wagner, CFA, Director, Research

Matt Wagner joined WisdomTree in May 2017 as an Analyst on the Research team. He currently serves as a Director, where he supports the creation, maintenance, and reconstitution of WisdomTree’s indexes and actively managed ETFs. Matt began his career at Morgan Stanley, working as an analyst in Treasury Capital Markets from 2015 to 2017, focusing on unsecured funding planning, execution, and risk management. He graduated magna cum laude from Boston College in 2015 with a B.A. in International Studies, concentrating in Economics. In 2020, he earned a Certificate in Advanced Valuation from NYU Stern. He is also a Chartered Financial Analyst (CFA) charterholder.

data.description:
• The fund posted returns of 1.32% (Institutional shares) and 1.25% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the third quarter of 2025.
  • Emerging market rates, U.S. high yield credit, and emerging market spreads contributed to performance, while developed and emerging market currencies and U.S. investment grade credit detracted.
  • The fund's underweight position in duration (interest rate sensitivity) was tactically reduced by increasing the allocations to U.S. and emerging market rates. Amid economic resilience, the fund increased its U.S. dollar and emerging market currency exposures, preferring to own select Asian currencies. It continued to hold an up-in-quality bias across credit sectors.

Contributors

The fund's overweight position in emerging market rates contributed as rates rallied due to easing central bank policies and trade deals. Similarly, emerging market spreads, particularly credit, was additive as spreads broadly tightened. An out-of-benchmark exposure to U.S. high yield credit contributed as spreads continued to tighten, given strong corporate earnings, economic resilience, and a resumption of the Federal Reserve's interest rate cuts.

Detractors

An overweight position in the U.S. dollar and underweight allocations to select non-U.S. developed market currencies detracted. The U.S. currency faced continued weakness due to tariff and fiscal deficit concerns. An underweight holding in the euro hampered performance as the currency appreciated amid higher growth expectations. In emerging market currencies, an underweight position in the Chinese yuan was unhelpful. Meanwhile, an underweight exposure to U.S. investment grade credit was a slight detractor as spreads broadly tightened.

Further insight

The underweight holding in top-line duration was decreased by reducing the underweight exposure to developed market rates and moving to an overweight position in emerging market rates. Within U.S. rates, the fund holds overweight positions in the “belly” and long end of the yield curve, given expectations that terminal rates may settle lower. Additionally, the fund holds an overweight position in emerging market rates, preferring countries with resilient growth and easing monetary policy. We increased the U.S. dollar and emerging market currency exposures, while decreasing the allocations to the euro and Japanese yen. We tactically rotated across spread sectors as we continued to seek attractive global sources of income while remaining cautious down the credit stack.

data.title: Western Asset Managed Municipals Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: During the third quarter, volatility in both equity and Treasury markets remained relatively subdued. The U.S. Treasury yield curve bull-steepened, reflecting market expectations for slower growth, moderation in economic data and potential rate cuts. Risk assets performed well, with the S&P 500 Index reaching new highs and credit spreads tightening. Municipal supply conditions continue at a record pace as municipal issuance reached $155 billion in 3Q25, up 12% from 3Q24 levels. Tax-exempt issuance fell 2% quarter-over-quarter (QoQ) to $145 billion and taxable issuance fell 40% QoQ to $10 billion. Year-to-date new-issue supply of $439 billion is 18% higher than the prior year, with tax-exempt issuance up 19% year-over-year and taxable issuance in line with prior year levels.
  • Contributors: Security selection in the power sector contributed to performance.
  • Detractors: Issue selection in the leasing sector detracted from performance.
  • Outlook: Muni fundamentals remained resilient. While strong revenue collections are indicative of credit resiliency in the muni market, the pace of credit rating improvement from the three major rating agencies has slowed so far this year.

Performance Review

  • Duration positioning contributed to performance.
  • Overweight to the industrial revenue sector contributed to performance.
  • Security selection in the power sector contributed to performance.
  • Issue selection to IL detracted from performance.
  • Issue selection in the leasing and local GO sectors detracted from performance.

Outlook

  • Western Asset believes longer maturities continue to offer attractive relative value for long-term investors, considering elevated absolute muni yield levels and rolldown opportunities from the curve which remains relatively steep versus taxable fixed income markets.
  • We anticipate that a strong labor market and consumer spending will support tax collections and municipal credit conditions over the medium term. However, we expect the potential for tax collections to be more critical for budgets if federal spending reductions extend more broadly to state and local budgets.

data.title: Janus Henderson Growth And Income Managed Account Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Portfolio returned 6.92% (gross) and the S&P 500® Index returned 8.12%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the communication services and energy sectors contributed to relative performance, while stock selection in information technology and consumer discretionary detracted.

Outlook

Substantial technology infrastructure investment and artificial intelligence (AI)-driven productivity gains support our positive economic outlook.

Investment environment

  • U.S. equities posted strong gains in the third quarter as corporate earnings growth remained robust and the economy expanded at 3.8% annual rate, ahead of earlier estimates. Personal income and consumption expenditures have been resilient despite slower-than-expected jobs growth.
  • The Federal Reserve (Fed) cut its policy rate by 25 basis points in September, and the market priced in at least one additional rate cut by year-end. Anticipation of lower rates boosted investor sentiment and equity valuations, though tariff uncertainty has created some headwinds.
  • Market performance reflected a return to mega cap concentration from earlier in the year as the top 50 stocks in the S&P 500 Index materially outperformed the equal-weighted index. Increased capital spending on AI infrastructure fueled the Mag 7 technology stocks and the broader tech sector. Dividend-oriented stocks lagged the broader market as high-multiple, non-dividend paying companies posted the strongest gains.

Portfolio review

In the third quarter, dividend stocks lagged the broader market. Our emphasis on high-quality, dividend-growth stocks hindered relative performance in a momentum-driven market led by non- or lower-dividend-paying mega-cap technology stocks.

In terms of individual stock performance, Lam Research was among top relative contributors. The semiconductor manufacturing equipment company benefited from growing optimism around AI infrastructure spending and wafer fabrication equipment demand. Lam has gained market share by positioning itself in higher growth segments and has recovered from a deeper valuation discount relative to peers.

Amphenol, another top contributor, continued to benefit from AI capacity growth. The cabling and connector company's networking business has expanded significantly as a key supplier of connectors for NVIDIA chips and servers, securing substantially higher content share in NVIDIA's latest Blackwell platform. The company also completed several high-return acquisitions that could be accretive to earnings.

Applied Materials (AMAT) detracted from relative performance. The semi-cap equipment maker has lost market share within the wafer fabrication equipment sector and has higher exposure to slower-growing customers. We exited the position and reallocated capital to semiconductor equipment names with stronger competitive positioning and exposure to AI-driven demand.

Consulting firm Accenture (ACN) was also a top detractor. The company experienced moderating organic growth as government-related business contracted due to federal spending cuts and IT budgets shifted from traditional consulting toward AI investments. This shift compressed Accenture's valuation as markets assess whether AI will disrupt people-based consulting businesses.

Manager outlook

We maintain an optimistic outlook on continued economic growth, supported by substantial technology infrastructure investment and productivity gains.

AI infrastructure spending is creating outsized economic impact. Technology capital expenditures now account for a substantial portion of U.S. real GDP growth and we expect these investments to continue. Given the importance of AI infrastructure investment to the economy, we are closely monitoring it to ensure companies are achieving appropriate returns, that backlogs are being executed, and that revenue supports those backlogs.

Beyond tech, recent tax changes allowing immediate depreciation of R&D and other investments are fueling a broader capital expenditure cycle. Many companies are accelerating depreciation to save money and reinvesting about half of those savings, creating additional spending across the market. This capital investment could drive productivity gains and support future economic growth.

A shift toward greater efficiency has become increasingly evident in operating margins, labor productivity data, and hiring trends. More companies are providing examples of AI applications and productivity gains, including headcount reductions. In the latest earnings season, operating leverage was stronger than expected, with companies posting healthy revenue growth while keeping expense growth in check. This productivity dynamic is boosting margins and supporting earnings growth despite tariff pressures and rising costs.

Encouragingly, second-quarter earnings strength extended beyond AI and technology. Commercial aerospace, capital markets, medical technology segments, and travel delivered impressive results. This breath suggests corporate health is more widespread than some narratives suggest.

From a consumer standpoint, we recognize recent job numbers have been softer in terms of labor force growth. However, the labor force is still expanding, and wage growth remains above pre-pandemic levels. We continue to be optimistic about consumer spending. Next year, consumer spending could get an additional boost from tax reform passed over the summer as filers see better refunds during tax season.

We recognize market multiples are higher than in recent history, but we believe that two factors justify current levels. First, the industry mix within major indices has shifted toward faster-growing sectors that typically command higher multiples. When you examine valuations within individual industries rather than the overall market, increases are more modest. Second, strong recent earnings growth with similar expectations for the remainder of this year and into next warrant higher valuations.

Our focus remains on companies providing attractive dividend yields and those that have the potential to grow dividends and earnings over time. We believe our emphasis on companies with consistent cash flows and healthy balance sheets can help buffer shareholder returns as we navigate economic and policy uncertainties.

data.title: Are Silver Equities Really Lagging The Silver Price?
data.description:
By Peter Bures

With the recent (and relatively consistent) daily/weekly silver price records, commentary has turned from "how short is XYZ bank, and how much trouble is Comex in" to "why are silver-related equities so cheap on a historic basis."

Being sceptics ourselves, we immediately had to ask: Are they, though?

So I want to explore whether or not the 'silver stocks are cheap' thesis is indeed defensible and, if so, what could 2026 potentially look like relative to this record-setting year.

Looking back 30 years, we see that silver is up 11x, while silver producers (Pan American Silver Corp. (PAAS), Hecla Mining Company (HL), and Coeur Mining, Inc. (CDE) – the three that were around thirty years ago) were up an unfortunate and quite laggy 3-fold (when index-weighted equally, starting from a baseline of one).

Since silver outperformed the share price by a factor of almost four, stocks must be cheap, right?

HL, CDE, and PAAS vs. Silver Price and Market Capitalization Per Ounce

Not so fast! The issue with rebasing and indexing share prices only reflects one variable – the price of the share of the company. What is missing is the number of shares the company has issued and outstanding after equity issues over the last 30 years. In each case, this is a large number after accounting for dilution.

Adjusting for this second variable by looking at market capitalization would be, in our opinion, a better reflection of the value of the company (as opposed to the company’s equity) vs. the price of silver.

Reflecting the capital structure in the relative valuation shows that the market is (gasp!) a lot better off reflecting the underlying value of the company than a casual observer would think.

With the advent of various index funds that claim to be silver (we’ll argue that less than 50% of revenues are actually derived from silver – more on that later), we can zoom in on a more recent timeframe.

The junior silver miner index (SILJ) launched shortly after the silver miner index (SIL) and shares a number of the same companies, so we’re combining the two in this study on an equal-weighted basis (i.e., 50% SIL and 50% SILJ).

As with the three longest-trading equities we picked above (and weighted 33.3/33.3/33.3), we compare the absolute index price and the market cap of the two indices to the price of silver and find that (Figure 2)...

SIL and SILJ vs. Silver and SIL+SILJ Market Cap per Oz

...unsurprisingly, the market cap of the indexes (reflective of the whole sector) is at or near all-time highs relative to the silver price, while the share prices lag behind.

The key variable, again, captured in this study, is the number of shares outstanding increasing faster than the silver price, resulting in share prices declining vs. silver while market capitalization is correctly at or near all-time highs.

Can share prices increase further with the silver price? Of course! However, managements' decisions to issue equity will have a negative/dilutive impact on the price of the shares.

If the market is correctly reflecting market cap per silver ounce (and by extension the share price of silver companies is not lagging), what can we expect from 2026?

In the last three decades of my career, I have seen prices of metals (gold, silver and copper) and producers outperform and underperform one another with macro- and microeconomic flows such as GDP growth-driven supply/demand, management missteps in executing on mine expansions, and orebodies not ‘hanging together.’

Ultimately, we have not seen a sustained pullback to below the all-in cost of production. Here, we are not using the commonly (yet non-IFRS) accepted 'all in sustainable costs,' or AISC, but rather simply taking the silver-equivalent production on a trailing basis (i.e., revenue divided by the average primary metal price over a certain period), subtracting cash from operations (prior to working cap adjustments), and dividing by the silver-equivalent production ounces.

This cleans up any byproduct or coproduct credits and simply rebases the total costs of operations to the silver-equivalent ounces produced.

Trailing three months, this number (all-in cost) is around $29.50/oz on a weighted basis (as per Bloomberg data). We would not expect any pullback below $30/oz to last very long – over the last 30 years a pullback below this ‘all-in cost’ never has. Not surprisingly, royalty companies’ all-in cost is $14/oz, so as a whole, the royalty sector should provide a safe haven in case there is a 50% drawdown in the silver price.

We don’t address names on an individual basis, but on a theoretical/practical basis, clearly there would be companies that lag on a market cap vs. silver basis (or vs. gold/copper/etc.) and those that are at all-time highs. Perhaps it is time to rotate some of the winners into the laggards?

Or perhaps that’s just wishful thinking, as SCRI trades at a discount to its 52-week high while essentially all other royalty companies (Table 1 below) trade at 52-week highs.

However, following our thesis above, almost all royalty companies (just like the indexed producers) are at/near their 52-wk highs, SCRI included. However, on a relative (i.e., vs. the silver price) basis, gaps do start to form with the larger cap companies outperforming the smaller caps – as may be expected with the early stages of capital rotation into a sector.

Royalty Company Price Performance

Despite being nowhere near all-time highs in terms of share price performance, silver producers (HL, CDE, PAAS, and those in the SIL and SILJ indices) are trading at/near their market capitalization highs adjusted for silver prices as a whole sector. Equity dilution is responsible for the apparently depressed share prices.

Rotation from leaders to laggards may be a strategy in 2026 (i.e., actual stock picking vs. general sector exposure). A pullback in silver (or other commodity prices) doesn’t last very long if it reached the ‘all-in’ production costs ($30/oz silver).

It's very difficult to defend the ‘mining stocks are cheap’ thesis – a rising tide has lifted almost all the boats; time to find out who’s swimming w/o a bathing suit.

All the best into 2026!

data.title: Columbia Seligman Global Technology Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Seligman Global Technology Fund returned 23.69% for the third quarter.
  • The fund's benchmark, the MSCI World Information Technology Index - Net (the index) returned 12.30% for the same period.
  • Stock selection within semiconductors and semiconductor equipment, as well as technology hardware storage & peripherals, contributed positively to performance during the quarter. The fund's off-benchmark allocation to industrials also contributed significantly to performance relative to the MSCI World Information Technology Index - Net. The fund's allocation to financials and stock selection within communication services detracted from relative returns.
  • For monthly performance, please visit columbiathreadneedleus.com .

Market overview

U.S. equities moved steadily higher during the third quarter, building on gains from the first six months of the year. The Russell 1000 Index — a measure of performance for large-cap stocks — rose 7.99%, boosting its year-to-date return to 14.60% as of September 30. Investors were cheered by a continuation of positive economic growth, receding fears about the possible impact of tariffs and robust second-quarter corporate earnings. A favorable shift in U.S. Federal Reserve policy provided an additional tailwind for the market. The Fed reduced rates by a quarter point at its September meeting, its first cut of 2025, and investors began to anticipate that further easing would be in store over the coming 12 months. This environment fueled a surge in investor appetite for risk, leading to a rally highlighted by a series of new all-time highs for the major indices.

Quarterly portfolio recap

  • Technology indices continued to move higher during the quarter, as evidenced by MSCI World Information Technology Index, which returned 12.30% during the third quarter. The primary driver of returns in the technology market has been companies indexed to artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure and the cloud, while other sectors of technology have struggled to keep pace. The rate of capital expenditure (CAPEX) spending remains high, as cloud providers have already committed to significant spending in 2026, on the back of historically high 2025 spend. U.S. cloud-data-center spending from Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon (AMZN), CoreWeave, Apple and Oracle (ORCL) is expected to exceed $400 billion in 2025 alone.
  • The fund maintained an underweight position in NVIDIA relative to its benchmark. The chip manufacturer represents a large allocation in the benchmark. The fund's underweight position detracted from performance relative to the benchmark as the company posted positive results and traded higher during the quarter, in line with other AI-indexed securities.
  • The portfolio's holding in semiconductor equipment manufacturer Lam Research contributed significantly to performance as the stock performed exceptionally well during the quarter and the fund maintained a significant relative overweight to the benchmark. Lam continues to be an AI beneficiary from a company perspective, as AI workloads require more advanced chips, which drives demand for Lam Research's deposition and etch solutions. The rebounding NAND flash market is also contributing to the growth of the company, as LAM's molybdenum and carbon gap fill solutions are continuing to gain traction. The company announced record-high profits and revenues on the company's most recent earnings call.
  • The fund continued to hold an off-benchmark position in Bloom Energy. The company manufactures and markets solid-oxide fuel cells that produce electricity and can provide an alternative source of energy compared to traditional supply. Our thesis on Bloom has been that it has the necessary technology to provide a solution to the electricity shortage that overhangs new AI data center builds in the U.S. and around the world. Bloom's fuel cells plug into a natural gas line that can fit on a data center's campus without taking up much real estate, and the company has informed customers they can ramp up power delivery quicker than other energy providers. Bloom received a favorable tax credit from the Trump administration's "One Big Beautiful Bill" which included a provision that supports hydrogen fuel cell and clean hydrogen production. This credit has led Bloom to approach cost parity with many other energy providers, including wind turbines. Add in the company's ability to deliver a solution quicker, and the current environment appears set up well for Bloom. In July, the company also announced a partnership with Oracle to provide support for Oracle's OCI data-center business and said they could be operational within 90-days - further emphasizing our thesis on Bloom's opportunity due to traditional energy backlogs.
  • The fund maintained a position in Apple throughout the quarter, at an underweight relative to the benchmark that detracted from fund performance. Apple was at the center of a few headlines during the quarter. In particular, the company received a ruling in its case with Google (GOOG) that Google can continue to pay Apple marketing fees to remain a search engine in the Safari browser, although not the exclusive search engine. Apple also benefitted from a meeting with President Trump in August in which he announced that Apple would be largely exempt from paying tariffs because the company has been investing in the United States. Within hardware, the fund also maintained an overweight position in Western Digital that helped relative performance versus the benchmark. Fundamentally, the company is well positioned in its space and should benefit from being a downstream AI beneficiary, and from the increased cost of memory. The company increased its margins and recently raised the prices of its disk drives for the first time in a few years, signaling continued strong demand for its products.

  • Within software, the fund's performance was mixed. Some large positions contributed to performance, but there were positions the fund owned at an overweight relative to the benchmark that ultimately detracted. The fund held an underweight position in Microsoft relative to the benchmark that contributed positively to performance as the stock moved slightly higher during the third quarter. The fund's position in cloud provider Oracle contributed to performance relative to the benchmark during the third quarter. Oracle saw a large run-up in its stock during the third quarter as the company announced that their remaining performance obligations jumped year over year and also announced new partnerships with Google and OpenAI during the quarter. The fund did not hold a position in Palantir Technologies (PLTR). This detracted overall from performance as the stock price rose during the quarter, driven by new contracts the company secured with the U.S. government, including the Department of Defense and Immigrations and Customs Enforcement. The fund also did not hold a position in AppLovin (APP), which detracted from returns relative to the index. AppLovin's stock moved higher during the quarter, fueled by optimism ahead of a product event that unveiled a self-serve ad platform aimed at e-commerce and other non-gaming advertisers.
  • The fund maintained a position in semiconductor company Broadcom. Broadcom's expertise in designing custom data-center chips, application-specific integrated circuits (ASIC) and auxiliary processing units (XPU) for major hyperscale customers like Google and Meta drove significant growth for the company during the third quarter. Broadcom also announced a new partnership with OpenAI that brings their four largest customers to Alphabet, Meta, Byte Dance and OpenAI. The fund's overweight position in semiconductor company Marvell Technology contributed to performance as Marvell's stock price continued to recover from April lows. The fund's allocations to Teradyne (TER) and Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) also contributed positively to performance, as the semiconductor industry as a whole continued to recover well behind subdued tariff news. The fund's overweight positions in ON Semiconductor (ON) and NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) detracted from performance relative to the benchmark as non-AI-indexed semiconductor companies continued to see a lag in performance relative to AI-indexed firms. The fund did not hold positions in Intel (INTC) or Micron Technology (MU), which detracted from performance as both names posted positive results during the quarter with their stock prices moving higher.

Outlook

The market is navigating significant crosscurrents of positive and negative trends, as we have seen the market continue to rebound off lows driven primarily by tariff announcements earlier this year. Positive market trends include:

  • Hyperscaler capex spending remains strong, and executives are committed to participating in the AI race.
  • Semiconductor chip demand remains robust, and the need for higher bandwidth memory has increased memory pricing, as AI demand levels are still elevated.
  • The need for robust cybersecurity solutions has become paramount as the focus on AI increases, and cybersecurity firms have been able to find some pockets of success despite overall volatility within technology.

The negatives include:

  • Information technology (IT) service firms are facing difficulties, as some investors expect AI to replace call-center employees and IT consultants.
  • Financial technology (Fintech) has struggled during recent quarters, due to fears of weakening consumer spending amid car-loan delinquencies and pressure from student loans becoming payable again.
  • Enterprise software continues to be negatively impacted by announcements from AI providers of new programs that could disrupt traditional software providers.

Our expectations for various sectors are as follows:

  • AI remains the strongest theme in technology, as infrastructure buildouts continue and companies hasten to deploy new AI capabilities. Capital spending by cloud service providers like Microsoft, Alphabet, Amazon, Oracle and Meta Platforms continues to grow.
  • Semiconductor equipment demand has continued to recover strongly during the second and third quarters behind quieting tariff news. Semiconductor equipment companies have been focused on reallocating production across geographic locations to adjust for potential impacts of tariffs once conversations restart.
  • Cybersecurity consistently remains a top priority for chief information officer (CIO) budgets, with limited impact from macro factors, and companies positioned here continue to have a favorable secular backdrop for sustained growth.
